                 AND
                 EARNINGS
                 AND MONTHLY
                 REPORT ON                                                           FEBRUARY 1968
                 THE LABOR FORCE                                                      VOL. 14 NO. 8
                  JOSEPH M. F1NERTY, EDITOR   KATHRYN D. HOYLE, ASSOCIATE EDITOR



                                                       Highlights

                               In accordance with regular practice at the beginning of
                               each year, the seasonally adjusted labor force series
                               have been slightly revised, due to the application of
                               new seasonal factors which incorporate 1967 data. The
                               revisions did not affect the total unemployment rates
                               published in 1967 by more than 0. 1 percentage point.
                               The method of seasonal adjustment, the new factors ,
                               and the updated seasonally adjusted series appear on
                               pages 11, 13, and 14-Z2.

                                            Labor Force Series

            In accordance with its regular practice at              The change in the seasonal pattern for
         the beginning of each year, the Bureau of               teenagers results from the addition of the
         Labor Statistics has recomputed and revised             "current availability" test for teenage job-
         the seasonal adjustment factors for unem-               seekers because, during the spring, persons
         ployment and other labor force series. The              in school looking for summer jobs are no
         revisions incorporate data through Decem-               longer counted as unemployed. In 1967, a
         ber 1967. The revised seasonal adjustment               special procedure was used to develop ap-
         factors shown on page 13 and the seasonally             propriate seasonal factors for unemployed
         adjusted series on pages 15-22 replace those            16 to 19 year-olds. This procedure was im-
         published in the February 1967 Employment               proved for use in 1968 and extended to cover
         and Earnings and Monthly Report on the                  the factors for 16 and 17 year-olds, 18 and
         Labor Force, which were based on data                   1 9 year-olds, and 16 to 19 year-olds by color,
         through December 1966. The incorporation                and the short-term unemployed (less than 5
         of 1967 data and the recomputation of the               weeks).
          seasonally adjusted data do not alter the un-
          employment rate of all civilian workers from              An evaluation and comparison of data
         those published last year by more than 09l              based on the new definitions with historical
         percentage point for any month in the entire            data based on the old definitions did not
          series.                                                clearly indicate any appreciable changes in
                                                                 the seasonal pattern for other series. None-

          measurement of employment and unemploy-

          ment were modified and improved in line

          Committee to Appraise Employment and Un-

          have clarified and sharpened concepts and              Historical Comparability

       The unemployment totals were virtually           the process except for the problem of ex-
       unchanged.                                       treme values. Most series contain some
                                                        values which do not quite fit the general
       Method of Adjustment                             pattern. The method uses a graduated ap-
                                                        proach in which every data value is assigned
          The seasonal adjustment method usedfor        a credence factor between zero and one.
       these series continues to be an adaptation of    Each credence factor is based on the size of
       the traditional ratio-to-moving-average          the irregular deviation for that value; small
       method, with allowance for changing sea-         deviations imply full credence, large devia-
       sonal patterns.                                  tions zero credence, and intermediate devia-
                                                        tions intermediate credence.
          The original data are regarded as a prod-
       uct of a trend-cycle component times a              The credence factors are used in two
       seasonal component'times an irregular com-       ways. They serve as auxiliary weights in the"
       ponent. The trend-cycle represents the           various moving averages so that the impact
       "real" movement of the series, including         of each aberrant data value is reduced in
       cyclical movements, The seasonal com-            accordance with its credence factor. They
       ponent is the annually repetitive pattern        are also used in computing modified original
       which makes certain months consistently          values, where the amount of modification
       higher or lower than others. The irregular       varies inversely with the credence factor.
       component is a residual, including sampling
       errors and short-term fluctuations which do         The process is repeated twice in order to
       not follow any consistent pattern. After a       refine the credence factors and the resulting
       satisfactory decomposition is achieved, the      decomposition. The effect of all this is to keep
       seasonally adjusted series is computed by        most .of the aberrations out of the trend-cycle
       dividing each original value by the corres-      and seasonal components and make them part
       ponding seasonal factor.                         of the irregular instead.

          A centered 12-month moving average of            Many of the labor force series are sea-
       the original data is used as the first esti-     sonally adjusted by aggregation rather than
       mate of the trend-cycle. This is divided into    directly. That is, parts of the labor force are
       the original value to provide seasonal-          adjusted directly and their seasonally ad-
       irregulars. A 7-term weighted moving aver-       justed values are then combined to provide
       age of these seasonal-irregulars for the         seasonally adjusted values for the aggre-

                                                       Technical Note

                                           Additional information concerning the preparation
                                           of the labor force, employment, hours and earnings,
                                           and labor turnover series—concepts and scope,
                                           survey methods, and limitations—is contained in
                                           technical notes for each of these series, available
                                           from the Bureau of Labor Statistics free of charge.



        INTRODUCTION                                                    Relation between the household and payroll series

         Labor Turnover                                                  ESTIMATING METHODS

             Labor turnover is the gross movement of wage and                The principal features of the procedure used to esti-
         salary workers into and out of employed status with             mate employment for the industry statistics are (1) the
         respect to individual establishments. This movement,            use of the "link relative" technique, which is a form of
         which relates to a calendar month, is divided into two          ratio estimation, (2) periodic adjustment of employ-
         broad types: Accessions (new hires and rehires) and             ment levels to new benchmarks, and (3) the use of size
         separations (terminations of employment initiated by            and regional stratification.
         either employer or employee). Each type of action is              The "Link Relative" Technique
         cumulated for a calendar month and expressed as a rate
         per 100 employees. The data relate to all employees,                From a sample composed of establishments reporting
         whether full- or part-time, permanent or temporary,             for both the previous and current months, the ratio of
         including executive, office, sales, other salaried per-         current month employment to that of the previous month
         sonnel, and production workers. Transfers to another            is computed. This is called a link relative. The estimates
         establishment of the company are included, beginning            of employment (all employees, including production and
         with January 1959.                                              nonproduction workers together) for the current month

        Design

            The sampling plan used in the current employment             Coverage
        statistics program is an optimum allocation design known             The BLS sample of establishment employment gnd
        as "sampling proportionate to average size of establish-         payrolls is the largest monthly sampling operation in the
        ment." The universe of establishments is stratified first        field of social statistics. The table that follows shows
        by industry and then within each industry by size of             the approximate proportion of total employment in each
        establishment in terms of employment. For each industry          industry division covered by the group of establishments
        the total size of the sample is distributed among the size       furnishing monthly employment data. The coverage for
        class cells on the basis of average employment per               individual industries within the division may vary from
        establishment in each cell. In practice, this is equivalent      the proportions shown.
        to distributing the predetermined total number of estab-
        lishments required in the sample among the cells on the
        basis of the ratio of employment in each cell to total              Approximate size and coverage of BLS employment
        employment in the industry. Within each stratum the                        and payrolls sample, March 1966 x
        sample members are selected at random.

            Under this type of design, large establishments fall                                                 Employees
        into the sample with certainty. The size of the samples                   Industry division

                                                          ATTENTION

              As discussed in the Technical Note, the Bureau periodically adjusts the industry employ-
        ment series to a recent benchmark to improve their accuracy. These adjustments may also
        affect the hours and earnings series because employment levels are used as weights. Industry
        data for all national series shown in this report have been adjusted to March 1966 benchmarks.
        Data from Apri,l 1966 forward are subject to revision at the time of the next benchmark,

              Beginning with the September 1967 and subsequent issues of Employment and Earnings
        and Monthly Report on the Labor Force, the national data in sections B, C, and D supersede
        those published in previous issues, as well as those appearing in the Handbook of Labor Statis-
        tics, 1967. Comparable data are published in Employment and Earnings Statistics for the
        United States, 1909-67, BLS Bulletin 1312-5.

                 Industry titles conform to the Bureau of the Budget's standard list of short SIC titles.
